title: Why React nags you about keys

mcq.question_md: You render a list of notices with `notices.map(n => <NoticeCard ... />)` and the console warns: *"Each child in a list should have a unique key prop."* What is the `key` actually for?
options: It sets the CSS `id` so each card can be styled separately
It lets React tell list items apart, so it updates the right ones when the list changes
It encrypts the item's data before rendering
It decides the order in which the items appear on screen
mcq.explanation_md: When the list changes — a notice gets deleted from the middle, say — React compares the new list with the old one to update as little of the page as possible. Keys are the name tags that make that comparison reliable: same key means same item, even if its position moved. Without stable keys (or with array indexes as keys), deleting item 2 can make React re-render or mis-match everything after it, and inputs inside list items can show the wrong values. Option D is the tempting one — keys look positional, but order comes from the array itself; the key only identifies *which* item is which.
